# Agent Development Kit (ADK) for Go [![License](https://img.shields.io/badge/License-Apache_2.0-blue.svg)](LICENSE) [![Go Doc](https://img.shields.io/badge/Go%20Package-Doc-blue.svg)](https://pkg.go.dev/google.golang.org/adk) [![Nightly Check](https://github.com/google/adk-go/actions/workflows/nightly.yml/badge.svg)](https://github.com/google/adk-go/actions/workflows/nightly.yml) [![r/agentdevelopmentkit](https://img.shields.io/badge/Reddit-r%2Fagentdevelopmentkit-FF4500?style=flat&logo=reddit&logoColor=white)](https://www.reddit.com/r/agentdevelopmentkit/) [![Ask DeepWiki](https://deepwiki.com/badge.svg)](https://deepwiki.com/google/adk-go)

An open-source, code-first Go toolkit for building, evaluating, and deploying sophisticated AI agents with flexibility and control.

Important Links: Docs & Samples & Python ADK & Java ADK & ADK Web.

Agent Development Kit (ADK) is a flexible and modular framework that applies software development principles to AI agent creation. It is designed to simplify building, deploying, and orchestrating agent workflows, from simple tasks to complex systems. While optimized for Gemini, ADK is model-agnostic, deployment-agnostic, and compatible with other frameworks. This Go version of ADK is ideal for developers building cloud-native agent applications, leveraging Go's strengths in concurrency and performance. --- ## 🆕 OpenAI Adapter for Local LLMs **This fork adds OpenAI-compatible adapter support**, enabling you to run ADK agents on: - 🖥️ **Local LLMs** (LM Studio, Ollama) - ☁️ **OpenAI API** (GPT-4, GPT-3.5-turbo) - 🔧 **Any OpenAI-compatible endpoint** ### ✨ Features - ✅ **Multi-turn tool calling** - Full conversation flow with tool execution - ✅ **Streaming responses** - Server-Sent Events (SSE) for real-time output - ✅ **Session management** - Automatic conversation history with TTL - ✅ **Error handling** - Exponential backoff, rate limiting, retry logic - ✅ **Comprehensive testing** - 146 tests, 74.8% coverage ### 🚀 Quick Start **1. Setup Local LLM** (LM Studio recommended) ```bash # Download LM Studio from https://lmstudio.ai/ # Load google/gemma-3-12b model # Start local server on port 1234 ``` **2. Run Example** ```bash cd examples/openai go build -o weather_agent main.go ./weather_agent console ``` **3. Try it** ``` > What's the weather in London? Agent: The weather in London is sunny with a temperature of 22°C... ``` ### 📦 Usage ```go import "google.golang.org/adk/model/openai" // Create OpenAI model adapter model, err := openai.NewModel("google/gemma-3-12b", &openai.Config{ BaseURL: "http://localhost:1234/v1", }) if err != nil { // Handle error } // Create agent with tools agent, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{ Name: "my_assistant", Model: model, Tools: []tool.Tool{/* your tools */}, }) if err != nil { // Handle error } ``` ### 🏗️ Architecture ``` model/openai/ ├── openai.go # Main adapter implementation ├── streaming.go # SSE streaming support ├── converters.go # ADK ↔ OpenAI format conversion ├── tool_executor.go # Tool execution engine ├── session.go # Session management

content.text:
The problem is that the fork (`github.com/jiatianzhao/adk-go-openai`) is a fork of `google.golang.org/adk` with a different module path. It won't work as a drop-in — you'd have to replace the entire ADK dependency. Instead, the cleanest approach is to vendor just the OpenAI adapter into this project since it only depends on the ADK `model.LLM` interface.

Let me check what that interface looks like in your current ADK version, and then plan the integration.

content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/pc/.claude/plugins/cache/superpowers-marketplace/superpowers/4.3.1/skills/brainstorming

# Brainstorming Ideas Into Designs

## Overview

Help turn ideas into fully formed designs and specs through natural collaborative dialogue.

Start by understanding the current project context, then ask questions one at a time to refine the idea. Once you understand what you're building, present the design and get user approval.

<HARD-GATE>
Do NOT invoke any implementation skill, write any code, scaffold any project, or take any implementation action until you have presented a design and the user has approved it. This applies to EVERY project regardless of perceived simplicity.
</HARD-GATE>

## Anti-Pattern: "This Is Too Simple To Need A Design"

Every project goes through this process. A todo list, a single-function utility, a config change — all of them. "Simple" projects are where unexamined assumptions cause the most wasted work. The design can be short (a few sentences for truly simple projects), but you MUST present it and get approval.

## Checklist

You MUST create a task for each of these items and complete them in order:

1. **Explore project context** — check files, docs, recent commits
2. **Ask clarifying questions** — one at a time, understand purpose/constraints/success criteria
3. **Propose 2-3 approaches** — with trade-offs and your recommendation
4. **Present design** — in sections scaled to their complexity, get user approval after each section
5. **Write design doc** — save to `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md` and commit
6. **Transition to implementation** — invoke writing-plans skill to create implementation plan

## Process Flow

```dot
digraph brainstorming {
    "Explore project context" [shape=box];
    "Ask clarifying questions" [shape=box];
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" [shape=box];
    "Present design sections" [shape=box];
    "User approves design?" [shape=diamond];
    "Write design doc" [shape=box];
    "Invoke writing-plans skill" [shape=doublecircle];

    "Explore project context" -> "Ask clarifying questions";
    "Ask clarifying questions" -> "Propose 2-3 approaches";
    "Propose 2-3 approaches" -> "Present design sections";
    "Present design sections" -> "User approves design?";
    "User approves design?" -> "Present design sections" [label="no, revise"];
    "User approves design?" -> "Write design doc" [label="yes"];
    "Write design doc" -> "Invoke writing-plans skill";
}
```

**The terminal state is invoking writing-plans.** Do NOT invoke frontend-design, mcp-builder, or any other implementation skill. The ONLY skill you invoke after brainstorming is writing-plans.

## The Process

**Understanding the idea:**
- Check out the current project state first (files, docs, recent commits)
- Ask questions one at a time to refine the idea
- Prefer multiple choice questions when possible, but open-ended is fine too
- Only one question per message - if a topic needs more exploration, break it into multiple questions
- Focus on understanding: purpose, constraints, success criteria

**Exploring approaches:**
- Propose 2-3 different approaches with trade-offs
- Present options conversationally with your recommendation and reasoning
- Lead with your recommended option and explain why

**Presenting the design:**
- Once you believe you understand what you're building, present the design
- Scale each section to its complexity: a few sentences if straightforward, up to 200-300 words if nuanced
- Ask after each section whether it looks right so far
- Cover: architecture, components, data flow, error handling, testing
- Be ready to go back and clarify if something doesn't make sense

## After the Design

**Documentation:**
- Write the validated design to `docs/plans/YYYY-MM-DD-<topic>-design.md`
- Use elements-of-style:writing-clearly-and-concisely skill if available
- Commit the design document to git

**Implementation:**
- Invoke the writing-plans skill to create a detailed implementation plan
- Do NOT invoke any other skill. writing-plans is the next step.

## Key Principles

- **One question at a time** - Don't overwhelm with multiple questions
- **Multiple choice preferred** - Easier to answer than open-ended when possible
- **YAGNI ruthlessly** - Remove unnecessary features from all designs
- **Explore alternatives** - Always propose 2-3 approaches before settling
- **Incremental validation** - Present design, get approval before moving on
- **Be flexible** - Go back and clarify when something doesn't make sense

1→package agent
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"encoding/json"
     6→	"fmt"
     7→	"log/slog"
     8→	"net/http"
     9→	"os"
    10→	"strings"
    11→	"time"
    12→
    13→	"google.golang.org/genai"
    14→
    15→	"google.golang.org/adk/agent"
    16→	"google.golang.org/adk/agent/llmagent"
    17→	"google.golang.org/adk/model/gemini"
    18→	"google.golang.org/adk/runner"
    19→	"google.golang.org/adk/session"
    20→	"google.golang.org/adk/tool"
    21→
    22→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/models"
    23→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/routing"
    24→)
    25→
    26→// OrchestratorStore defines all data access methods required by the agent
    27→// orchestrator to load project configuration, run the agent, and persist
    28→// the resulting semantic release.
    29→type OrchestratorStore interface {
    30→	AgentDataStore
    31→	GetProject(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.Project, error)
    32→	GetAgentRun(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.AgentRun, error)
    33→	UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx context.Context, id, status string) error
    34→	CreateSemanticRelease(ctx context.Context, sr *models.SemanticRelease, releaseIDs []string) error
    35→	UpdateAgentRunResult(ctx context.Context, id string, semanticReleaseID string) error
    36→	ListProjectSubscriptions(ctx context.Context, projectID string) ([]models.Subscription, error)
    37→	GetChannel(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.NotificationChannel, error)
    38→}
    39→
    40→const defaultModel = "gemini-2.5-flash"
    41→
    42→const defaultInstruction = `You are a release intelligence agent for a software project.
    43→Your job is to analyze recent upstream releases from the project's tracked sources
    44→and produce a structured semantic release report.
    45→
    46→Use the available tools to:
    47→1. Fetch the list of recent releases for this project.
    48→2. Inspect individual release details (changelogs, commit data, raw payloads).
    49→3. Review the project's context sources (runbooks, documentation) for background.
    50→
    51→CRITICAL: Your final response MUST be a single JSON object and nothing else.
    52→Do not include any explanation, commentary, or markdown formatting — just the raw JSON.
    53→
    54→The JSON object must have exactly these fields:
    55→{
    56→  "summary": "A 2-3 sentence high-level summary of what changed across the releases.",
    57→  "availability": "Are these releases available and stable? (e.g., 'GA', 'RC', 'Beta')",
    58→  "adoption": "What is the recommended adoption timeline? (e.g., 'Immediate', 'Next sprint', 'Monitor')",
    59→  "urgency": "How urgent is upgrading? (e.g., 'Critical', 'High', 'Medium', 'Low')",
    60→  "recommendation": "A concrete 1-2 sentence recommendation for the team."
    61→}`
    62→
    63→// Orchestrator manages the lifecycle of an agent run: loading project config,
    64→// creating an ADK-Go agent with project-specific tools and instructions,
    65→// running the agent, and persisting the result as a semantic release.
    66→type Orchestrator struct {
    67→	store  OrchestratorStore
    68→	apiKey string
    69→}
    70→
    71→// NewOrchestrator creates a new Orchestrator. It reads GOOGLE_API_KEY from the
    72→// environment. If the key is missing, it returns an error so the caller can
    73→// degrade gracefully (log a warning, skip registering the agent worker).
    74→func NewOrchestrator(store OrchestratorStore) (*Orchestrator, error) {
    75→	apiKey := os.Getenv("GOOGLE_API_KEY")
    76→	if apiKey == "" {
    77→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("GOOGLE_API_KEY is not set; agent orchestrator requires an LLM API key")
    78→	}
    79→	return &Orchestrator{
    80→		store:  store,
    81→		apiKey: apiKey,
    82→	}, nil
    83→}
    84→
    85→// RunAgent executes the full agent lifecycle for a given agent run:
    86→//  1. Marks the run as "running".
    87→//  2. Loads the project and builds the agent prompt.
    88→//  3. Creates an ADK-Go LLM agent with project-scoped tools.
    89→//  4. Runs the agent and captures its final text response.
    90→//  5. Parses the response into a SemanticReport.
    91→//  6. Creates a SemanticRelease and links it to the agent run.
    92→//  7. Marks the run as "completed" (or "failed" on error).
    93→func (o *Orchestrator) RunAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) error {
    94→	// Mark as running.
    95→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "running"); err != nil {
    96→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to running: %w", err)
    97→	}
    98→
    99→	// Execute the agent; capture any error to mark the run as failed.
   100→	result, err := o.executeAgent(ctx, run)
   101→	if err != nil {
   102→		slog.Error("agent run failed", "run_id", run.ID, "err", err)
   103→		// Best-effort: mark the run as failed.
   104→		if statusErr := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "failed"); statusErr != nil {
   105→			slog.Error("agent: failed to mark run as failed",
   106→				"run_id", run.ID, "status_err", statusErr)
   107→		}
   108→		return err
   109→	}
   110→
   111→	// Link the semantic release to the agent run and mark completed.
   112→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunResult(ctx, run.ID, result.semanticReleaseID); err != nil {
   113→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run result: %w", err)
   114→	}
   115→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "completed"); err != nil {
   116→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to completed: %w", err)
   117→	}
   118→
   119→	// Send project-level notifications (best-effort; errors are logged, not returned).
   120→	o.sendProjectNotifications(ctx, run, result)
   121→
   122→	return nil
   123→}
   124→
   125→// agentResult holds the output of a successful agent execution, used to
   126→// construct notifications after the semantic release is persisted.
   127→type agentResult struct {
   128→	semanticReleaseID string
   129→	version           string
   130→	reportText        string
   131→	projectName       string
   132→}
   133→
   134→// executeAgent performs the actual LLM agent interaction and returns the
   135→// agentResult containing the semantic release ID and metadata for notifications.
   136→func (o *Orchestrator) executeAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) (*agentResult, error) {
   137→	// Load project.
   138→	slog.Info("agent: loading project", "run_id", run.ID, "project_id", run.ProjectID)
   139→	project, err := o.store.GetProject(ctx, run.ProjectID)
   140→	if err != nil {
   141→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("get project: %w", err)
   142→	}
   143→	slog.Info("agent: project loaded", "run_id", run.ID, "project", project.Name)
   144→
   145→	// Build instruction from project's agent_prompt or use default.
   146→	instruction := defaultInstruction
   147→	if project.AgentPrompt != "" {
   148→		instruction = project.AgentPrompt + "\n\n" + defaultInstruction
   149→		slog.Debug("agent: using custom agent prompt", "run_id", run.ID)
   150→	}
   151→
   152→	// Create the Gemini model.
   153→	slog.Info("agent: creating LLM model", "run_id", run.ID, "model", defaultModel)
   154→	llmModel, err := gemini.NewModel(ctx, defaultModel, &genai.ClientConfig{
   155→		APIKey: o.apiKey,
   156→	})
   157→	if err != nil {
   158→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create LLM model: %w", err)
   159→	}
   160→
   161→	// Create project-scoped tools.
   162→	slog.Debug("agent: creating tools", "run_id", run.ID, "project_id", run.ProjectID)
   163→	tools, err := NewTools(o.store, run.ProjectID)
   164→	if err != nil {
   165→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create agent tools: %w", err)
   166→	}
   167→
   168→	// Create the ADK-Go LLM agent.
   169→	slog.Debug("agent: creating ADK agent", "run_id", run.ID)
   170→	agentInstance, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
   171→		Name:        "release_analyst",
   172→		Description: "Analyzes upstream releases and produces semantic release reports.",
   173→		Model:       llmModel,
   174→		Instruction: instruction,
   175→		Tools:       tools,
   176→	})
   177→	if err != nil {
   178→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create LLM agent: %w", err)
   179→	}
   180→
   181→	// Create in-memory session service and a new session.
   182→	sessionService := session.InMemoryService()
   183→	createResp, err := sessionService.Create(ctx, &session.CreateRequest{
   184→		AppName: "changelogue",
   185→		UserID:  "system",
   186→	})
   187→	if err != nil {
   188→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create session: %w", err)
   189→	}
   190→	sess := createResp.Session
   191→
   192→	// Create the runner.
   193→	r, err := runner.New(runner.Config{
   194→		AppName:        "changelogue",
   195→		Agent:          agentInstance,
   196→		SessionService: sessionService,
   197→	})
   198→	if err != nil {
   199→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create runner: %w", err)
   200→	}
   201→
   202→	// Run the agent with a prompt requesting analysis.
   203→	slog.Info("agent: starting LLM run", "run_id", run.ID, "project", project.Name)
   204→	userMsg := genai.NewContentFromText(
   205→		"Analyze the recent releases for this project and produce a semantic release report.",
   206→		"user",
   207→	)
   208→
   209→	var finalText string
   210→	eventCount := 0
   211→	for event, err := range r.Run(ctx, "system", sess.ID(), userMsg, agent.RunConfig{}) {
   212→		if err != nil {
   213→			slog.Error("agent: run event error",
   214→				"run_id", run.ID,
   215→				"event_count", eventCount,
   216→				"err", err,
   217→			)
   218→			return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent run event error: %w", err)
   219→		}
   220→		eventCount++
   221→		if event != nil && event.IsFinalResponse() && event.Content != nil {
   222→			for _, part := range event.Content.Parts {
   223→				if part.Text != "" {
   224→					finalText += part.Text
   225→				}
   226→			}
   227→		}
   228→	}
   229→	slog.Info("agent: LLM run finished",
   230→		"run_id", run.ID,
   231→		"event_count", eventCount,
   232→		"output_length", len(finalText),
   233→	)
   234→
   235→	if finalText == "" {
   236→		slog.Error("agent: produced no output",
   237→			"run_id", run.ID,
   238→			"project_id", run.ProjectID,
   239→			"event_count", eventCount,
   240→		)
   241→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("agent produced no output")
   242→	}
   243→
   244→	// Parse the agent's response into a SemanticReport.
   245→	report, err := parseReport(finalText)
   246→	if err != nil {
   247→		slog.Warn("agent output was not valid JSON report, storing raw",
   248→			"run_id", run.ID, "parse_err", err, "raw_output", finalText)
   249→		// Fall back to storing the raw text as the summary.
   250→		report = &models.SemanticReport{
   251→			Summary: finalText,
   252→		}
   253→	}
   254→
   255→	reportJSON, err := json.Marshal(report)
   256→	if err != nil {
   257→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("marshal report: %w", err)
   258→	}
   259→
   260→	// Gather release IDs for the semantic_release_sources join table.
   261→	releases, _, err := o.store.ListReleasesByProject(ctx, run.ProjectID, 1, 50)
   262→	if err != nil {
   263→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("list releases for semantic release: %w", err)
   264→	}
   265→	releaseIDs := make([]string, 0, len(releases))
   266→	for _, r := range releases {
   267→		releaseIDs = append(releaseIDs, r.ID)
   268→	}
   269→
   270→	// Determine a version label from the most recent release.
   271→	version := "unknown"
   272→	if len(releases) > 0 {
   273→		version = releases[0].Version
   274→	}
   275→
   276→	now := time.Now()
   277→	sr := &models.SemanticRelease{
   278→		ProjectID:   run.ProjectID,
   279→		Version:     version,
   280→		Report:      reportJSON,
   281→		Status:      "completed",
   282→		CompletedAt: &now,
   283→	}
   284→
   285→	if err := o.store.CreateSemanticRelease(ctx, sr, releaseIDs); err != nil {
   286→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("create semantic release: %w", err)
   287→	}
   288→
   289→	slog.Info("agent run produced semantic release",
   290→		"run_id", run.ID,
   291→		"semantic_release_id", sr.ID,
   292→		"version", sr.Version,
   293→	)
   294→
   295→	return &agentResult{
   296→		semanticReleaseID: sr.ID,
   297→		version:           version,
   298→		reportText:        finalText,
   299→		projectName:       project.Name,
   300→	}, nil
   301→}
   302→
   303→// defaultSenders returns a map of channel type to Sender for the supported
   304→// notification channel types (webhook, slack, discord).
   305→func defaultSenders() map[string]routing.Sender {
   306→	httpClient := &http.Client{Timeout: 10 * time.Second}
   307→	return map[string]routing.Sender{
   308→		"webhook": &routing.WebhookSender{Client: httpClient},
   309→		"slack":   &routing.SlackSender{Client: httpClient},
   310→		"discord": &routing.DiscordSender{Client: httpClient},
   311→	}
   312→}
   313→
   314→// sendProjectNotifications looks up project-level subscriptions and sends a
   315→// notification to each channel with the semantic release report. This is
   316→// best-effort: errors are logged but do not propagate to the caller.
   317→func (o *Orchestrator) sendProjectNotifications(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun, result *agentResult) {
   318→	subs, err := o.store.ListProjectSubscriptions(ctx, run.ProjectID)
   319→	if err != nil {
   320→		slog.Error("list project subscriptions for notification",
   321→			"project_id", run.ProjectID, "err", err)
   322→		return
   323→	}
   324→	if len(subs) == 0 {
   325→		slog.Debug("agent: no project subscriptions configured",
   326→			"project_id", run.ProjectID)
   327→		return
   328→	}
   329→
   330→	senders := defaultSenders()
   331→
   332→	msg := routing.Notification{
   333→		Title:   fmt.Sprintf("Semantic Release Report: %s %s", result.projectName, result.version),
   334→		Body:    result.reportText,
   335→		Version: result.version,
   336→	}
   337→
   338→	for _, sub := range subs {
   339→		ch, err := o.store.GetChannel(ctx, sub.ChannelID)
   340→		if err != nil {
   341→			slog.Error("get channel for project notification",
   342→				"channel_id", sub.ChannelID, "err", err)
   343→			continue
   344→		}
   345→
   346→		sender, ok := senders[ch.Type]
   347→		if !ok {
   348→			slog.Warn("unknown channel type for project notification", "type", ch.Type)
   349→			continue
   350→		}
   351→
   352→		if err := sender.Send(ctx, ch, msg); err != nil {
   353→			slog.Error("send project notification failed",
   354→				"channel", ch.Name, "type", ch.Type, "err", err)
   355→		} else {
   356→			slog.Info("project notification sent",
   357→				"channel", ch.Name, "project_id", run.ProjectID, "version", result.version)
   358→		}
   359→	}
   360→}
   361→
   362→// parseReport attempts to parse the agent's text output as a SemanticReport JSON.
   363→// It handles cases where the agent wraps the JSON in markdown code blocks.
   364→func parseReport(text string) (*models.SemanticReport, error) {
   365→	// Strip markdown code fences if present.
   366→	cleaned := strings.TrimSpace(text)
   367→	if strings.HasPrefix(cleaned, "```") {
   368→		// Remove opening fence (with optional language tag).
   369→		if idx := strings.Index(cleaned, "\n"); idx != -1 {
   370→			cleaned = cleaned[idx+1:]
   371→		}
   372→		// Remove closing fence.
   373→		if idx := strings.LastIndex(cleaned, "```"); idx != -1 {
   374→			cleaned = cleaned[:idx]
   375→		}
   376→		cleaned = strings.TrimSpace(cleaned)
   377→	}
   378→
   379→	var report models.SemanticReport
   380→	if err := json.Unmarshal([]byte(cleaned), &report); err != nil {
   381→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("parse report JSON: %w", err)
   382→	}
   383→
   384→	if report.Summary == "" {
   385→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("report is missing required 'summary' field")
   386→	}
   387→
   388→	return &report, nil
   389→}
   390→
   391→// toolsToSlice is a helper to convert the tool.Tool interface slice for use
   392→// with llmagent.Config. This is kept for API compatibility in case the
   393→// function signature changes.
   394→func toolsToSlice(tools []tool.Tool) []tool.Tool {
   395→	return tools
   396→}
   397→

1→package main
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"log/slog"
     6→	"net/http"
     7→	"os"
     8→	"os/signal"
     9→	"time"
    10→
    11→	"github.com/riverqueue/river"
    12→	agentpkg "github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/agent"
    13→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/api"
    14→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/db"
    15→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/ingestion"
    16→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/queue"
    17→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/routing"
    18→)
    19→
    20→func main() {
    21→	ctx, stop := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt)
    22→	defer stop()
    23→
    24→	dbURL := envOr("DATABASE_URL", "postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable")
    25→	addr := envOr("LISTEN_ADDR", ":8080")
    26→	noAuth := os.Getenv("NO_AUTH") == "true"
    27→
    28→	// Database
    29→	pool, err := db.NewPool(ctx, dbURL)
    30→	if err != nil {
    31→		slog.Error("database connection failed", "err", err)
    32→		os.Exit(1)
    33→	}
    34→	defer pool.Close()
    35→
    36→	if err := db.RunMigrations(ctx, pool); err != nil {
    37→		slog.Error("migrations failed", "err", err)
    38→		os.Exit(1)
    39→	}
    40→
    41→	// API & routing store (created before River so we can register workers)
    42→	// The river client is set to nil initially; TriggerAgentRun (which needs it)
    43→	// won't be called until the server is up and the client is set below.
    44→	pgStore := api.NewPgStore(pool, nil)
    45→
    46→	// Register River workers
    47→	workers := river.NewWorkers()
    48→	notifyWorker := routing.NewNotifyWorker(pgStore)
    49→	river.AddWorker(workers, notifyWorker)
    50→
    51→	// Agent worker: requires GOOGLE_API_KEY. If unavailable, agent jobs
    52→	// will remain in the queue until the key is configured and the server
    53→	// is restarted.
    54→	agentOrchestrator, err := agentpkg.NewOrchestrator(pgStore)
    55→	if err != nil {
    56→		slog.Warn("agent orchestrator not available — agent jobs will not be processed", "err", err)
    57→	} else {
    58→		agentWorker := agentpkg.NewAgentWorker(agentOrchestrator, pgStore)
    59→		river.AddWorker(workers, agentWorker)
    60→		slog.Info("agent worker registered")
    61→	}
    62→
    63→	riverClient, err := queue.NewRiverClient(pool, workers)
    64→	if err != nil {
    65→		slog.Error("river client failed", "err", err)
    66→		os.Exit(1)
    67→	}
    68→
    69→	// Now that the river client exists, re-create pgStore with it so that
    70→	// TriggerAgentRun can enqueue agent jobs.
    71→	pgStore = api.NewPgStore(pool, riverClient)
    72→
    73→	if err := riverClient.Start(ctx); err != nil {
    74→		slog.Error("river start failed", "err", err)
    75→		os.Exit(1)
    76→	}
    77→
    78→	// Ingestion layer
    79→	ingestionStore := ingestion.NewPgStore(pool, riverClient)
    80→	svc := ingestion.NewService(ingestionStore)
    81→
    82→	loader := ingestion.NewSourceLoader(pool, http.DefaultClient)
    83→	orch := ingestion.NewOrchestrator(svc, loader, pool, 5*time.Minute)
    84→
    85→	broadcaster := api.NewBroadcaster()
    86→
    87→	mux := http.NewServeMux()
    88→
    89→	// Register all API v1 routes
    90→	api.RegisterRoutes(mux, api.Dependencies{
    91→		DB:                    pool,
    92→		ProjectsStore:         pgStore,
    93→		ReleasesStore:         pgStore,
    94→		SubscriptionsStore:    pgStore,
    95→		SourcesStore:          pgStore,
    96→		ChannelsStore:         pgStore,
    97→		ContextSourcesStore:   pgStore,
    98→		SemanticReleasesStore: pgStore,
    99→		AgentStore:            pgStore,
   100→		KeyStore:              pgStore,
   101→		HealthChecker:         pgStore,
   102→		Broadcaster:           broadcaster,
   103→		NoAuth:                noAuth,
   104→	})
   105→
   106→	srv := &http.Server{Addr: addr, Handler: api.CORS(mux)}
   107→
   108→	// Start polling in background
   109→	go orch.Run(ctx)
   110→
   111→	// Start PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE bridge
   112→	go api.ListenForNotifications(ctx, pool, broadcaster)
   113→
   114→	// Start HTTP server
   115→	go func() {
   116→		slog.Info("server starting", "addr", srv.Addr)
   117→		if err := srv.ListenAndServe(); err != http.ErrServerClosed {
   118→			slog.Error("server error", "err", err)
   119→		}
   120→	}()
   121→
   122→	<-ctx.Done()
   123→	slog.Info("shutting down")
   124→
   125→	shutdownCtx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 10*time.Second)
   126→	defer cancel()
   127→	riverClient.Stop(shutdownCtx)
   128→	srv.Shutdown(shutdownCtx)
   129→}
   130→
   131→func envOr(key, fallback string) string {
   132→	if v := os.Getenv(key); v != "" {
   133→		return v
   134→	}
   135→	return fallback
   136→}
   137→

1→package agent
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"fmt"
     6→	"log/slog"
     7→
     8→	"github.com/riverqueue/river"
     9→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/queue"
    10→)
    11→
    12→// AgentWorker is a River worker that processes AgentJobArgs by running the
    13→// LLM agent orchestrator. When a job is dequeued, it loads the corresponding
    14→// agent run from the store and delegates to Orchestrator.RunAgent.
    15→type AgentWorker struct {
    16→	river.WorkerDefaults[queue.AgentJobArgs]
    17→	orchestrator *Orchestrator
    18→	store        OrchestratorStore
    19→}
    20→
    21→// NewAgentWorker creates a new AgentWorker backed by the given orchestrator
    22→// and store.
    23→func NewAgentWorker(orchestrator *Orchestrator, store OrchestratorStore) *AgentWorker {
    24→	return &AgentWorker{
    25→		orchestrator: orchestrator,
    26→		store:        store,
    27→	}
    28→}
    29→
    30→// Work processes a single AgentJobArgs job. It loads the agent run from the
    31→// database and runs the LLM agent through the orchestrator.
    32→func (w *AgentWorker) Work(ctx context.Context, job *river.Job[queue.AgentJobArgs]) error {
    33→	slog.Info("agent worker picked up job",
    34→		"job_id", job.ID,
    35→		"agent_run_id", job.Args.AgentRunID,
    36→		"attempt", job.Attempt,
    37→	)
    38→
    39→	run, err := w.store.GetAgentRun(ctx, job.Args.AgentRunID)
    40→	if err != nil {
    41→		slog.Error("agent worker failed to load agent run",
    42→			"job_id", job.ID,
    43→			"agent_run_id", job.Args.AgentRunID,
    44→			"err", err,
    45→		)
    46→		return fmt.Errorf("get agent run: %w", err)
    47→	}
    48→
    49→	slog.Info("agent worker starting run",
    50→		"job_id", job.ID,
    51→		"agent_run_id", run.ID,
    52→		"project_id", run.ProjectID,
    53→		"trigger", run.Trigger,
    54→	)
    55→
    56→	if err := w.orchestrator.RunAgent(ctx, run); err != nil {
    57→		slog.Error("agent worker run failed",
    58→			"job_id", job.ID,
    59→			"agent_run_id", run.ID,
    60→			"project_id", run.ProjectID,
    61→			"err", err,
    62→		)
    63→		return err
    64→	}
    65→
    66→	slog.Info("agent worker run completed",
    67→		"job_id", job.ID,
    68→		"agent_run_id", run.ID,
    69→		"project_id", run.ProjectID,
    70→	)
    71→	return nil
    72→}
    73→

Perfect. Now I have a complete understanding. Let me provide a comprehensive analysis:

## Analysis: Gemini Model Integration in ReleaseBeacon

### 1. **How Gemini Model is Currently Created**

**In `NewOrchestrator` (lines 74-83):**
- Reads `GOOGLE_API_KEY` from environment only
- Returns an error if the key is missing (graceful degradation)
- Stores the API key in the `Orchestrator` struct for later use

**In `executeAgent` (lines 152-159):**
- Creates a Gemini model using `gemini.NewModel()` from the ADK-Go library
- Uses a hardcoded model: **`gemini-2.5-flash`** (line 40)
- Provides API key via `genai.ClientConfig`
- The model instance is created fresh for each agent run

### 2. **Environment Variables That Control the Model**

Currently **only one environment variable**:
- **`GOOGLE_API_KEY`** — Google Gemini API key (required for agent operation)

No environment variable controls which Gemini model version is used (hardcoded to `gemini-2.5-flash`).

### 3. **How the Model is Passed to llmagent.Config**

At lines 170-176, the Gemini model is passed directly to `llmagent.Config`:

```go
agentInstance, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
    Name:        "release_analyst",
    Description: "Analyzes upstream releases and produces semantic release reports.",
    Model:       llmModel,              // ← Gemini model instance here
    Instruction: instruction,
    Tools:       tools,
})
```

The `llmagent.New()` function accepts the model as an `llmModel` interface, which the ADK-Go library uses to invoke predictions and tool use.

### 4. **What Would Need to Change to Support an Alternative Model Provider**

#### Option A: **Minimal Change — Support Multiple Gemini Models**
- Add env var: `LLM_MODEL` (default: `gemini-2.5-flash`)
- Read it in `executeAgent`, pass to `gemini.NewModel(ctx, modelName, ...)`
- **Impact:** 5 lines total

#### Option B: **Support Alternative Providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)**
Several design decisions required:

1. **Model Abstraction**
   - ADK-Go's `Model` interface is provider-agnostic
   - Other providers (OpenAI, Anthropic Claude) have community ADK-Go implementations
   - Could switch implementations without changing `llmagent` logic

2. **Environment Configuration**
   - Add env vars: `LLM_PROVIDER` (default: `gemini`), `LLM_MODEL`, `LLM_API_KEY`
   - Or: separate keys per provider (`GOOGLE_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`)

3. **Factory Pattern**
   - Create `NewLLMModel(ctx, provider, modelName, apiKey)` function
   - Switch on provider, instantiate appropriate model adapter
   - Location: new file `internal/agent/model.go`

4. **Required Code Changes**
   - Modify `NewOrchestrator()` to accept multiple API keys or read from config
   - Modify `executeAgent()` to call factory instead of hardcoded `gemini.NewModel()`
   - Update `cmd/server/main.go` initialization

5. **ADK-Go Compatibility**
   - Must verify chosen provider has working ADK-Go model implementation
   - Test with tool-use functionality (agent needs to call tools)

#### Option C: **Config File Approach**
- Read from `config.yaml` or similar
- Decouple env var chaos; more maintainable for complex scenarios
- Could support multiple projects with different models

Perfect. Now I have a complete understanding. Let me provide a comprehensive analysis:

## Analysis: Gemini Model Integration in ReleaseBeacon

### 1. **How Gemini Model is Currently Created**

**In `NewOrchestrator` (lines 74-83):**
- Reads `GOOGLE_API_KEY` from environment only
- Returns an error if the key is missing (graceful degradation)
- Stores the API key in the `Orchestrator` struct for later use

**In `executeAgent` (lines 152-159):**
- Creates a Gemini model using `gemini.NewModel()` from the ADK-Go library
- Uses a hardcoded model: **`gemini-2.5-flash`** (line 40)
- Provides API key via `genai.ClientConfig`
- The model instance is created fresh for each agent run

### 2. **Environment Variables That Control the Model**

Currently **only one environment variable**:
- **`GOOGLE_API_KEY`** — Google Gemini API key (required for agent operation)

No environment variable controls which Gemini model version is used (hardcoded to `gemini-2.5-flash`).

### 3. **How the Model is Passed to llmagent.Config**

At lines 170-176, the Gemini model is passed directly to `llmagent.Config`:

```go
agentInstance, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
    Name:        "release_analyst",
    Description: "Analyzes upstream releases and produces semantic release reports.",
    Model:       llmModel,              // ← Gemini model instance here
    Instruction: instruction,
    Tools:       tools,
})
```

The `llmagent.New()` function accepts the model as an `llmModel` interface, which the ADK-Go library uses to invoke predictions and tool use.

### 4. **What Would Need to Change to Support an Alternative Model Provider**

#### Option A: **Minimal Change — Support Multiple Gemini Models**
- Add env var: `LLM_MODEL` (default: `gemini-2.5-flash`)
- Read it in `executeAgent`, pass to `gemini.NewModel(ctx, modelName, ...)`
- **Impact:** 5 lines total

#### Option B: **Support Alternative Providers (OpenAI, Anthropic, etc.)**
Several design decisions required:

1. **Model Abstraction**
   - ADK-Go's `Model` interface is provider-agnostic
   - Other providers (OpenAI, Anthropic Claude) have community ADK-Go implementations
   - Could switch implementations without changing `llmagent` logic

2. **Environment Configuration**
   - Add env vars: `LLM_PROVIDER` (default: `gemini`), `LLM_MODEL`, `LLM_API_KEY`
   - Or: separate keys per provider (`GOOGLE_API_KEY`, `OPENAI_API_KEY`, `ANTHROPIC_API_KEY`)

3. **Factory Pattern**
   - Create `NewLLMModel(ctx, provider, modelName, apiKey)` function
   - Switch on provider, instantiate appropriate model adapter
   - Location: new file `internal/agent/model.go`

4. **Required Code Changes**
   - Modify `NewOrchestrator()` to accept multiple API keys or read from config
   - Modify `executeAgent()` to call factory instead of hardcoded `gemini.NewModel()`
   - Update `cmd/server/main.go` initialization

5. **ADK-Go Compatibility**
   - Must verify chosen provider has working ADK-Go model implementation
   - Test with tool-use functionality (agent needs to call tools)

#### Option C: **Config File Approach**
- Read from `config.yaml` or similar

    14→
    15→// Package model defines the interfaces and data structures for interacting with LLMs.
    16→package model
    17→
    18→import (
    19→	"context"
    20→	"iter"
    21→
    22→	"google.golang.org/genai"
    23→)
    24→
    25→// LLM provides the access to the underlying LLM.
    26→type LLM interface {
    27→	Name() string
    28→	GenerateContent(ctx context.Context, req *LLMRequest, stream bool) iter.Seq2[*LLMResponse, error]
    29→}
    30→
    31→// LLMRequest is the raw LLM request.
    32→type LLMRequest struct {
    33→	Model    string
    34→	Contents []*genai.Content
    35→	Config   *genai.GenerateContentConfig
    36→
    37→	Tools map[string]any `json:"-"`
    38→}
    39→
    40→// LLMResponse is the raw LLM response.
    41→// It provides the first candidate response from the model if available.
    42→type LLMResponse struct {
    43→	Content           *genai.Content
    44→	CitationMetadata  *genai.CitationMetadata
    45→	GroundingMetadata *genai.GroundingMetadata
    46→	UsageMetadata     *genai.GenerateContentResponseUsageMetadata
    47→	CustomMetadata    map[string]any
    48→	LogprobsResult    *genai.LogprobsResult
    49→	// Partial indicates whether the content is part of a unfinished content stream.
    50→	// Only used for streaming mode and when the content is plain text.
    51→	// The Runner fully processes only the final non-partial event, partial
    52→	// events are simply forwarded downstream (eg. to UI for display).
    53→	Partial bool
    54→	// Indicates whether the response from the model is complete.
    55→	// Only used for streaming mode.
    56→	TurnComplete bool
    57→	// Flag indicating that LLM was interrupted when generating the content.
    58→	// Usually it is due to user interruption during a bidi streaming.
    59→	Interrupted  bool
    60→	ErrorCode    string
    61→	ErrorMessage string
    62→	FinishReason genai.FinishReason
    63→	AvgLogprobs  float64
    64→}
    65→

1→package agent
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"encoding/json"
     6→	"fmt"
     7→	"log/slog"
     8→	"net/http"
     9→	"os"
    10→	"strings"
    11→	"time"
    12→
    13→	"google.golang.org/genai"
    14→
    15→	"google.golang.org/adk/agent"
    16→	"google.golang.org/adk/agent/llmagent"
    17→	"google.golang.org/adk/model/gemini"
    18→	"google.golang.org/adk/runner"
    19→	"google.golang.org/adk/session"
    20→	"google.golang.org/adk/tool"
    21→
    22→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/models"
    23→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/routing"
    24→)
    25→
    26→// OrchestratorStore defines all data access methods required by the agent
    27→// orchestrator to load project configuration, run the agent, and persist
    28→// the resulting semantic release.
    29→type OrchestratorStore interface {
    30→	AgentDataStore
    31→	GetProject(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.Project, error)
    32→	GetAgentRun(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.AgentRun, error)
    33→	UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx context.Context, id, status string) error
    34→	CreateSemanticRelease(ctx context.Context, sr *models.SemanticRelease, releaseIDs []string) error
    35→	UpdateAgentRunResult(ctx context.Context, id string, semanticReleaseID string) error
    36→	ListProjectSubscriptions(ctx context.Context, projectID string) ([]models.Subscription, error)
    37→	GetChannel(ctx context.Context, id string) (*models.NotificationChannel, error)
    38→}
    39→
    40→const defaultModel = "gemini-2.5-flash"
    41→
    42→const defaultInstruction = `You are a release intelligence agent for a software project.
    43→Your job is to analyze recent upstream releases from the project's tracked sources
    44→and produce a structured semantic release report.
    45→
    46→Use the available tools to:
    47→1. Fetch the list of recent releases for this project.
    48→2. Inspect individual release details (changelogs, commit data, raw payloads).
    49→3. Review the project's context sources (runbooks, documentation) for background.
    50→
    51→CRITICAL: Your final response MUST be a single JSON object and nothing else.
    52→Do not include any explanation, commentary, or markdown formatting — just the raw JSON.
    53→
    54→The JSON object must have exactly these fields:
    55→{
    56→  "summary": "A 2-3 sentence high-level summary of what changed across the releases.",
    57→  "availability": "Are these releases available and stable? (e.g., 'GA', 'RC', 'Beta')",
    58→  "adoption": "What is the recommended adoption timeline? (e.g., 'Immediate', 'Next sprint', 'Monitor')",
    59→  "urgency": "How urgent is upgrading? (e.g., 'Critical', 'High', 'Medium', 'Low')",
    60→  "recommendation": "A concrete 1-2 sentence recommendation for the team."
    61→}`
    62→
    63→// Orchestrator manages the lifecycle of an agent run: loading project config,
    64→// creating an ADK-Go agent with project-specific tools and instructions,
    65→// running the agent, and persisting the result as a semantic release.
    66→type Orchestrator struct {
    67→	store  OrchestratorStore
    68→	apiKey string
    69→}
    70→
    71→// NewOrchestrator creates a new Orchestrator. It reads GOOGLE_API_KEY from the
    72→// environment. If the key is missing, it returns an error so the caller can
    73→// degrade gracefully (log a warning, skip registering the agent worker).
    74→func NewOrchestrator(store OrchestratorStore) (*Orchestrator, error) {
    75→	apiKey := os.Getenv("GOOGLE_API_KEY")
    76→	if apiKey == "" {
    77→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("GOOGLE_API_KEY is not set; agent orchestrator requires an LLM API key")
    78→	}
    79→	return &Orchestrator{
    80→		store:  store,
    81→		apiKey: apiKey,
    82→	}, nil
    83→}
    84→
    85→// RunAgent executes the full agent lifecycle for a given agent run:
    86→//  1. Marks the run as "running".
    87→//  2. Loads the project and builds the agent prompt.
    88→//  3. Creates an ADK-Go LLM agent with project-scoped tools.
    89→//  4. Runs the agent and captures its final text response.
    90→//  5. Parses the response into a SemanticReport.
    91→//  6. Creates a SemanticRelease and links it to the agent run.
    92→//  7. Marks the run as "completed" (or "failed" on error).
    93→func (o *Orchestrator) RunAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) error {
    94→	// Mark as running.
    95→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "running"); err != nil {
    96→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to running: %w", err)
    97→	}
    98→
    99→	// Execute the agent; capture any error to mark the run as failed.
   100→	result, err := o.executeAgent(ctx, run)
   101→	if err != nil {
   102→		slog.Error("agent run failed", "run_id", run.ID, "err", err)
   103→		// Best-effort: mark the run as failed.
   104→		if statusErr := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "failed"); statusErr != nil {
   105→			slog.Error("agent: failed to mark run as failed",
   106→				"run_id", run.ID, "status_err", statusErr)
   107→		}
   108→		return err
   109→	}
   110→
   111→	// Link the semantic release to the agent run and mark completed.
   112→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunResult(ctx, run.ID, result.semanticReleaseID); err != nil {
   113→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run result: %w", err)
   114→	}
   115→	if err := o.store.UpdateAgentRunStatus(ctx, run.ID, "completed"); err != nil {
   116→		return fmt.Errorf("update agent run status to completed: %w", err)
   117→	}
   118→
   119→	// Send project-level notifications (best-effort; errors are logged, not returned).
   120→	o.sendProjectNotifications(ctx, run, result)
   121→
   122→	return nil
   123→}
   124→
   125→// agentResult holds the output of a successful agent execution, used to
   126→// construct notifications after the semantic release is persisted.
   127→type agentResult struct {
   128→	semanticReleaseID string
   129→	version           string
   130→	reportText        string
   131→	projectName       string
   132→}
   133→
   134→// executeAgent performs the actual LLM agent interaction and returns the
   135→// agentResult containing the semantic release ID and metadata for notifications.
   136→func (o *Orchestrator) executeAgent(ctx context.Context, run *models.AgentRun) (*agentResult, error) {
   137→	// Load project.
   138→	slog.Info("agent: loading project", "run_id", run.ID, "project_id", run.ProjectID)
   139→	project, err := o.store.GetProject(ctx, run.ProjectID)
   140→	if err != nil {
   141→		return nil, fmt.Errorf("get project: %w", err)
   142→	}
   143→	slog.Info("agent: project loaded", "run_id", run.ID, "project", project.Name)
   144→
   145→	// Build instruction from project's agent_prompt or use default.
   146→	instruction := defaultInstruction
   147→	if project.AgentPrompt != "" {
   148→		instruction = project.AgentPrompt + "\n\n" + defaultInstruction
   149→		slog.Debug("agent: using custom agent prompt", "run_id", run.ID)
   150→	}

   226→			}
   227→		}
   228→	}

Perfect! Now I have all the information needed. Let me compile a comprehensive report.

## Analysis: model.LLM Interface from Google ADK-Go v0.5.0

I've successfully located and analyzed the `model.LLM` interface from the Google ADK-Go module cached at your system. Here's the complete breakdown:

### 1. **The model.LLM Interface Definition**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/go/pkg/mod/google.golang.org/adk@v0.5.0/model/llm.go`

The `LLM` interface requires two methods:

go
```go
type LLM interface {
    Name() string
    GenerateContent(ctx context.Context, req *LLMRequest, stream bool) iter.Seq2[*LLMResponse, error]
}
```

**Methods:**
- **`Name() string`** — Returns the name of the LLM model (e.g., "gemini-2.5-flash")
- **`GenerateContent(ctx context.Context, req *LLMRequest, stream bool) iter.Seq2[*LLMResponse, error]`** — Core method that generates content. Uses Go 1.22's iterator protocol (`iter.Seq2`) to yield responses with errors. Returns an iterator instead of a single response, supporting both streaming and non-streaming modes.

### 2. **LLMRequest Type**

```go
type LLMRequest struct {
    Model    string                              // Model name
    Contents []*genai.Content                    // Conversation messages
    Config   *genai.GenerateContentConfig       // Configuration (temperature, top_p, etc.)
    Tools    map[string]any `json:"-"`          // Tool definitions (not JSON serialized)
}
```

**Key Notes:**
- `Contents` holds the conversation history and prompts
- `Config` contains generation parameters from the underlying `genai` package
- `Tools` maps tool names to their definitions (used by agents)

### 3. **LLMResponse Type**

```go
type LLMResponse struct {
    Content           *genai.Content                              // The generated content from model
    CitationMetadata  *genai.CitationMetadata                     // Citation sources (for grounding)
    GroundingMetadata *genai.GroundingMetadata                    // Grounding info
    UsageMetadata     *genai.GenerateContentResponseUsageMetadata // Token counts
    CustomMetadata    map[string]any                              // Custom metadata
    LogprobsResult    *genai.LogprobsResult                       // Log probability data
    Partial           bool                                        // True for streaming: content is unfinished
    TurnComplete      bool                                        // Streaming: response generation complete
    Interrupted       bool                                        // Streaming: generation was interrupted
    ErrorCode         string                                      // Error classification (e.g., "SAFETY")
    ErrorMessage      string                                      // Human-readable error
    FinishReason      genai.FinishReason                          // Why generation stopped (STOP, SAFETY, etc.)
    AvgLogprobs       float64                                     // Average log probability
}
```

**Streaming Notes:**
- `Partial=true` indicates unfinished content in streaming mode
- `TurnComplete=true` marks the final event of a turn
- `Interrupted=true` means the user stopped generation

### 4. **gemini.NewModel Implementation**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/go/pkg/mod/google.golang.org/adk@v0.5.0/model/gemini/gemini.go`

The factory function returns a `model.LLM` backed by the Gemini API:

```go
func NewModel(ctx context.Context, modelName string, cfg *genai.ClientConfig) (model.LLM, error)
```

**Internal Structure (geminiModel):**
```go
type geminiModel struct {
    client             *genai.Client
    name               string
    versionHeaderValue string  // "google-adk/VERSION gl-go/VERSION"
}
```

**Implementation Details:**
1. Creates a `genai.Client` using the provided `ClientConfig` (which contains `APIKey`)
2. Wraps it with version header tracking
3. Returns the `geminiModel` struct which satisfies `model.LLM`

**GenerateContent Implementation:**
- For `stream=false`: Calls `m.client.Models.GenerateContent()` synchronously  
- For `stream=true`: Calls `m.client.Models.GenerateContentStream()` and uses an internal `StreamingResponseAggregator` to process partial chunks
- Both modes wrap results in the `iter.Seq2` iterator pattern
- Automatically appends user content if the request messages end with a non-user role (to keep model responsive)

### 5. **Real Usage in ReleaseBeacon**

Your project uses this in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/agent/orchestrator.go`:

```go
llmModel, err := gemini.NewModel(ctx, defaultModel, &genai.ClientConfig{
    APIKey: o.apiKey,  // GOOGLE_API_KEY from env
})
if err != nil {
    return nil, fmt.Errorf("create LLM model: %w", err)
}

// Passed to ADK agent:
agentInstance, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
    Name:        "release_analyst",
    Model:       llmModel,        // Implements model.LLM
    Instruction: instruction,
    Tools:       tools,
})
```

The agent framework uses the `LLM` interface to make requests, which the `geminiModel` translates to Gemini API calls via the `genai` package.

### Summary

The `model.LLM` interface is a clean abstraction layer that decouples ADK-Go's agent framework from specific LLM providers. The iterator-based `GenerateContent` method elegantly handles both streaming and non-streaming scenarios. The `gemini.NewModel` implementation wires the Gemini API client into this interface, enabling your release analysis agents to call Gemini models.

```go
    CitationMetadata  *genai.CitationMetadata                     // Citation sources (for grounding)
    GroundingMetadata *genai.GroundingMetadata                    // Grounding info
    UsageMetadata     *genai.GenerateContentResponseUsageMetadata // Token counts
    CustomMetadata    map[string]any                              // Custom metadata
    LogprobsResult    *genai.LogprobsResult                       // Log probability data
    Partial           bool                                        // True for streaming: content is unfinished
    TurnComplete      bool                                        // Streaming: response generation complete
    Interrupted       bool                                        // Streaming: generation was interrupted
    ErrorCode         string                                      // Error classification (e.g., "SAFETY")
    ErrorMessage      string                                      // Human-readable error
    FinishReason      genai.FinishReason                          // Why generation stopped (STOP, SAFETY, etc.)
    AvgLogprobs       float64                                     // Average log probability
}
```

**Streaming Notes:**
- `Partial=true` indicates unfinished content in streaming mode
- `TurnComplete=true` marks the final event of a turn
- `Interrupted=true` means the user stopped generation

### 4. **gemini.NewModel Implementation**

**Location:** `/Users/pc/go/pkg/mod/google.golang.org/adk@v0.5.0/model/gemini/gemini.go`

The factory function returns a `model.LLM` backed by the Gemini API:

```go
func NewModel(ctx context.Context, modelName string, cfg *genai.ClientConfig) (model.LLM, error)
```

**Internal Structure (geminiModel):**
```go
type geminiModel struct {
    client             *genai.Client
    name               string
    versionHeaderValue string  // "google-adk/VERSION gl-go/VERSION"
}
```

**Implementation Details:**
1. Creates a `genai.Client` using the provided `ClientConfig` (which contains `APIKey`)
2. Wraps it with version header tracking
3. Returns the `geminiModel` struct which satisfies `model.LLM`

**GenerateContent Implementation:**
- For `stream=false`: Calls `m.client.Models.GenerateContent()` synchronously  
- For `stream=true`: Calls `m.client.Models.GenerateContentStream()` and uses an internal `StreamingResponseAggregator` to process partial chunks
- Both modes wrap results in the `iter.Seq2` iterator pattern
- Automatically appends user content if the request messages end with a non-user role (to keep model responsive)

### 5. **Real Usage in ReleaseBeacon**

Your project uses this in `/Users/pc/web3/ReleaseBeacon/internal/agent/orchestrator.go`:

```go
llmModel, err := gemini.NewModel(ctx, defaultModel, &genai.ClientConfig{
    APIKey: o.apiKey,  // GOOGLE_API_KEY from env
})
if err != nil {
    return nil, fmt.Errorf("create LLM model: %w", err)
}

// Passed to ADK agent:
agentInstance, err := llmagent.New(llmagent.Config{
    Name:        "release_analyst",
    Model:       llmModel,        // Implements model.LLM
    Instruction: instruction,
    Tools:       tools,
})
```

The agent framework uses the `LLM` interface to make requests, which the `geminiModel` translates to Gemini API calls via the `genai` package.

### Summary

The `model.LLM` interface is a clean abstraction layer that decouples ADK-Go's agent framework from specific LLM providers. The iterator-based `GenerateContent` method elegantly handles both streaming and non-streaming scenarios. The `gemini.NewModel` implementation wires the Gemini API client into this interface, enabling your release analysis agents to call Gemini models.

content.text:
Here's the design:

## Design: OpenAI Model Support

### Architecture

A new `internal/agent/openai/` package containing a lightweight adapter (~200 lines) that implements `model.LLM` from `google.golang.org/adk`. It translates ADK's `genai.Content` messages to OpenAI chat completion format and back, supporting tool calls.

### Components

1. **`internal/agent/openai/openai.go`** — Adapter implementing `model.LLM` with `Name()` and `GenerateContent()`. Handles:
   - `genai.Content` -> OpenAI messages conversion
   - Tool definitions conversion
   - OpenAI API HTTP calls
   - Response -> `model.LLMResponse` conversion

2. **`internal/agent/model.go`** — Factory function `NewLLMModel(ctx, provider, model, config)` that returns `model.LLM`, switching between `gemini.NewModel()` and `openai.NewModel()`.

3. **`internal/agent/orchestrator.go`** — Modified to:
   - Accept provider/model/config at construction time
   - Call the factory instead of hardcoded `gemini.NewModel()`

### Environment Variables

| Variable | Default | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `LLM_PROVIDER` | `gemini` | `gemini` or `openai` |
| `LLM_MODEL` | `gemini-2.5-flash` | Model name (e.g. `gpt-5.2`) |
| `OPENAI_API_KEY` | (empty) | OpenAI API key |
| `OPENAI_BASE_URL` | `https://api.openai.com/v1` | Custom endpoint |
| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | (empty) | Gemini API key (existing) |

### Data Flow

```
Orchestrator.executeAgent()
  → NewLLMModel(provider, model)  // factory
    → if "openai": openai.NewModel(model, baseURL, apiKey)
    → if "gemini": gemini.NewModel(ctx, model, clientConfig)
  → llmagent.New(Config{Model: llmModel})  // same as today
  → runner.Run()  // ADK handles tool loop
```

Does this look right, or would you change anything?

1→package main
     2→
     3→import (
     4→	"context"
     5→	"log/slog"
     6→	"net/http"
     7→	"os"
     8→	"os/signal"
     9→	"time"
    10→
    11→	"github.com/riverqueue/river"
    12→	agentpkg "github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/agent"
    13→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/api"
    14→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/db"
    15→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/ingestion"
    16→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/queue"
    17→	"github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue/internal/routing"
    18→)
    19→
    20→func main() {
    21→	ctx, stop := signal.NotifyContext(context.Background(), os.Interrupt)
    22→	defer stop()
    23→
    24→	dbURL := envOr("DATABASE_URL", "postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable")
    25→	addr := envOr("LISTEN_ADDR", ":8080")
    26→	noAuth := os.Getenv("NO_AUTH") == "true"
    27→
    28→	// Database
    29→	pool, err := db.NewPool(ctx, dbURL)
    30→	if err != nil {
    31→		slog.Error("database connection failed", "err", err)
    32→		os.Exit(1)
    33→	}
    34→	defer pool.Close()
    35→
    36→	if err := db.RunMigrations(ctx, pool); err != nil {
    37→		slog.Error("migrations failed", "err", err)
    38→		os.Exit(1)
    39→	}
    40→
    41→	// API & routing store (created before River so we can register workers)
    42→	// The river client is set to nil initially; TriggerAgentRun (which needs it)
    43→	// won't be called until the server is up and the client is set below.
    44→	pgStore := api.NewPgStore(pool, nil)
    45→
    46→	// Register River workers
    47→	workers := river.NewWorkers()
    48→	notifyWorker := routing.NewNotifyWorker(pgStore)
    49→	river.AddWorker(workers, notifyWorker)
    50→
    51→	// Agent worker: requires GOOGLE_API_KEY. If unavailable, agent jobs
    52→	// will remain in the queue until the key is configured and the server
    53→	// is restarted.
    54→	agentOrchestrator, err := agentpkg.NewOrchestrator(pgStore)
    55→	if err != nil {
    56→		slog.Warn("agent orchestrator not available — agent jobs will not be processed", "err", err)
    57→	} else {
    58→		agentWorker := agentpkg.NewAgentWorker(agentOrchestrator, pgStore)
    59→		river.AddWorker(workers, agentWorker)
    60→		slog.Info("agent worker registered")
    61→	}
    62→
    63→	riverClient, err := queue.NewRiverClient(pool, workers)
    64→	if err != nil {
    65→		slog.Error("river client failed", "err", err)
    66→		os.Exit(1)
    67→	}
    68→
    69→	// Now that the river client exists, re-create pgStore with it so that
    70→	// TriggerAgentRun can enqueue agent jobs.
    71→	pgStore = api.NewPgStore(pool, riverClient)
    72→
    73→	if err := riverClient.Start(ctx); err != nil {
    74→		slog.Error("river start failed", "err", err)
    75→		os.Exit(1)
    76→	}
    77→
    78→	// Ingestion layer
    79→	ingestionStore := ingestion.NewPgStore(pool, riverClient)
    80→	svc := ingestion.NewService(ingestionStore)
    81→
    82→	loader := ingestion.NewSourceLoader(pool, http.DefaultClient)
    83→	orch := ingestion.NewOrchestrator(svc, loader, pool, 5*time.Minute)
    84→
    85→	broadcaster := api.NewBroadcaster()
    86→
    87→	mux := http.NewServeMux()
    88→
    89→	// Register all API v1 routes
    90→	api.RegisterRoutes(mux, api.Dependencies{
    91→		DB:                    pool,
    92→		ProjectsStore:         pgStore,
    93→		ReleasesStore:         pgStore,
    94→		SubscriptionsStore:    pgStore,
    95→		SourcesStore:          pgStore,
    96→		ChannelsStore:         pgStore,
    97→		ContextSourcesStore:   pgStore,
    98→		SemanticReleasesStore: pgStore,
    99→		AgentStore:            pgStore,
   100→		KeyStore:              pgStore,
   101→		HealthChecker:         pgStore,
   102→		Broadcaster:           broadcaster,
   103→		NoAuth:                noAuth,
   104→	})
   105→
   106→	srv := &http.Server{Addr: addr, Handler: api.CORS(mux)}
   107→
   108→	// Start polling in background
   109→	go orch.Run(ctx)
   110→
   111→	// Start PostgreSQL LISTEN/NOTIFY → SSE bridge
   112→	go api.ListenForNotifications(ctx, pool, broadcaster)
   113→
   114→	// Start HTTP server
   115→	go func() {
   116→		slog.Info("server starting", "addr", srv.Addr)
   117→		if err := srv.ListenAndServe(); err != http.ErrServerClosed {
   118→			slog.Error("server error", "err", err)
   119→		}
   120→	}()
   121→
   122→	<-ctx.Done()
   123→	slog.Info("shutting down")
   124→
   125→	shutdownCtx, cancel := context.WithTimeout(context.Background(), 10*time.Second)
   126→	defer cancel()
   127→	riverClient.Stop(shutdownCtx)
   128→	srv.Shutdown(shutdownCtx)
   129→}
   130→
   131→func envOr(key, fallback string) string {
   132→	if v := os.Getenv(key); v != "" {
   133→		return v
   134→	}
   135→	return fallback
   136→}
   137→

But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

content.content:
1→# CLAUDE.md
     2→
     3→This file provides guidance to Claude Code (claude.ai/code) when working with code in this repository.
     4→
     5→## Project Overview
     6→
     7→**Changelogue** is an agent-driven release intelligence platform that polls upstream registries (Docker Hub, GitHub) for new releases, sends source-level notifications, and uses LLM agents (ADK-Go) to produce semantic release reports.
     8→
     9→Go module: `github.com/sentioxyz/changelogue`
    10→
    11→## Tech Stack
    12→
    13→- **Backend:** Go 1.25 — polling engine, notification routing, API server
    14→- **Database/Queue/PubSub:** PostgreSQL + [River](https://github.com/riverqueue/river) v0.31.0 for job queue (`FOR UPDATE SKIP LOCKED`), native `LISTEN`/`NOTIFY` for real-time events
    15→- **Frontend:** Next.js (React) + Tailwind CSS — dashboard (`web/`)
    16→- **Agent Intelligence:** [Google ADK-Go](https://google.github.io/adk-go/) v0.5.0 for agent orchestration — Gemini-powered semantic release analysis
    17→- **Deployment:** Single binary — Go `//go:embed` serves Next.js static export
    18→
    19→## Build & Test Commands
    20→
    21→```bash
    22→# Build
    23→go build -o changelogue ./cmd/server
    24→
    25→# Test all packages
    26→go test ./...
    27→
    28→# Test a single package
    29→go test ./internal/ingestion/...
    30→go test -v -run TestDockerHubSource ./internal/ingestion/...
    31→
    32→# Vet
    33→go vet ./...
    34→
    35→# Integration tests (requires Docker)
    36→bash scripts/integration-test.sh
    37→```
    38→
    39→## Local Development
    40→
    41→```bash
    42→# Start Postgres (docker-compose)
    43→make up
    44→
    45→# Run server (builds + starts with NO_AUTH, requires Postgres)
    46→make run
    47→
    48→# One command: start Postgres + run server
    49→make dev
    50→
    51→# Reset database
    52→make db-reset
    53→
    54→# Frontend
    55→make frontend-install
    56→make frontend-dev
    57→
    58→# Integration tests (spins up its own Postgres on port 5433)
    59→make integration-test
    60→
    61→# Cleanup everything (binary + containers + volumes)
    62→make clean
    63→```
    64→
    65→## Environment Variables
    66→
    67→| Variable | Default | Purpose |
    68→|----------|---------|---------|
    69→| `DATABASE_URL` | `postgres://localhost:5432/changelogue?sslmode=disable` | PostgreSQL connection |
    70→| `LISTEN_ADDR` | `:8080` | HTTP server bind address |
    71→| `GOOGLE_API_KEY` | (empty) | Gemini API key for agent LLM (agent worker disabled if unset) |
    72→
    73→## Architecture
    74→
    75→Four decoupled layers communicate exclusively through PostgreSQL:
    76→
    77→1. **Ingestion Layer** — Polling workers behind `IIngestionSource` interface discover new releases from upstream registries
    78→2. **Notification Routing** — River `NotifyWorker` sends source-level notifications to subscribed channels on new releases
    79→3. **Agent Layer** — ADK-Go agent (`internal/agent/`) researches releases via project-scoped tools and produces semantic release reports
    80→4. **Routing & Output** — Notification channels (Slack, Discord, webhooks) behind `Sender` interface
    81→
    82→### Transactional Outbox Pattern
    83→
    84→The critical data integrity pattern: release insert + River job enqueue happen in the same SQL transaction (`internal/ingestion/pgstore.go`). If either fails, both rollback — zero-loss guarantee.
    85→
    86→### Key Interfaces
    87→
    88→- `IIngestionSource` (`internal/ingestion/source.go`) — Polling-based providers implement `FetchNewReleases(ctx) ([]IngestionResult, error)`
    89→- `ReleaseStore` (`internal/ingestion/store.go`) — Persistence: `IngestRelease(ctx, sourceID string, result *IngestionResult) error`
    90→- `Sender` (`internal/routing/sender.go`) — Notification channel output: `Send(ctx, channel, msg) error`
    91→- `NotifyStore` (`internal/routing/worker.go`) — Data access for the notification worker
    92→- `AgentDataStore` (`internal/agent/tools.go`) — Data access for agent tools (releases, context sources)
    93→- `OrchestratorStore` (`internal/agent/orchestrator.go`) — Full data access for agent orchestrator (project, agent runs, semantic releases)
    94→
    95→### Data Flow
    96→
    97→```
    98→IIngestionSource.FetchNewReleases() → IngestionResult
    99→    → Service.ProcessResults() processes results
   100→    → PgStore.IngestRelease() in single TX: INSERT release + River InsertTx(NotifyJobArgs)
   101→    → NotifyWorker picks up job → sends source notifications + checks agent rules
   102→    → If rules match → enqueue AgentJobArgs
   103→    → AgentWorker picks up → runs ADK-Go agent → creates semantic release
   104→    → Sends project notifications
   105→```
   106→
   107→## Database
   108→
   109→Schema lives in `internal/db/migrations.go` (idempotent, runs on startup). Current tables:
   110→- `projects` — tracked software projects (central entity)
   111→- `sources` — configured ingestion sources per project (provider, repository, poll interval)
   112→- `context_sources` — read-only references for agent research (runbooks, docs)
   113→- `releases` — source-level releases, unique on `(source_id, version)`
   114→- `semantic_releases` — AI-generated project-level reports, unique on `(project_id, version)`
   115→- `semantic_release_sources` — join table linking semantic releases to source releases
   116→- `notification_channels` — standalone channels (webhook, Slack, Discord)
   117→- `subscriptions` — source-level and project-level notification subscriptions
   118→- `agent_runs` — agent execution records scoped to a project
   119→- `api_keys` — API authentication keys
   120→- River's own tables (created by `rivermigrate`)
   121→
   122→## Key Design References
   123→
   124→- `ARCH.md` — Full architecture: system diagram, data flow lifecycle
   125→- `DESIGN.md` — Component design: database schema, agent workflow, error handling
   126→
   127→## Workflow Orchestration
   128→
   129→### 1. Plan Node Default
   130→- Enter plan mode for ANY non-trivial task (3+ steps or architectural decisions)
   131→- If something goes sideways, STOP and re-plan immediately — don't keep pushing
   132→- Use plan mode for verification steps, not just building
   133→- Write detailed specs upfront to reduce ambiguity
   134→
   135→### 2. Subagent Strategy
   136→- Use subagents liberally to keep main context window clean
   137→- Offload research, exploration, and parallel analysis to subagents
   138→- For complex problems, throw more compute at it via subagents
   139→- One task per subagent for focused execution
   140→
   141→### 3. Self-Improvement Loop
   142→- After ANY correction from the user: update `docs/plans/lessons.md` with the pattern
   143→- Write rules for yourself that prevent the same mistake
   144→- Ruthlessly iterate on these lessons until mistake rate drops
   145→- Review lessons at session start for relevant project
   146→
   147→### 4. Verification Before Done
   148→- Never mark a task complete without proving it works
   149→- Diff behavior between main and your changes when relevant
   150→- Ask yourself: "Would a staff engineer approve this?"
   151→- Run tests, check logs, demonstrate correctness
   152→
   153→### 5. Demand Elegance (Balanced)
   154→- For non-trivial changes: pause and ask "is there a more elegant way?"
   155→- If a fix feels hacky: "Knowing everything I know now, implement the elegant solution"
   156→- Skip this for simple, obvious fixes — don't over-engineer
   157→- Challenge your own work before presenting it
   158→
   159→### 6. Autonomous Bug Fixing
   160→- When given a bug report: just fix it. Don't ask for hand-holding
   161→- Point at logs, errors, failing tests — then resolve them
   162→- Zero context switching required from the user
   163→- Go fix failing CI tests without being told how
   164→
   165→## Task Management
   166→
   167→1. **Plan First**: Write plan to `docs/plans/todo.md` with checkable items
   168→2. **Verify Plan**: Check in before starting implementation
   169→3. **Track Progress**: Mark items complete as you go
   170→4. **Explain Changes**: High-level summary at each step
   171→5. **Document Results**: Add review section to `docs/plans/todo.md`
   172→6. **Capture Lessons**: Update `docs/plans/lessons.md` after corrections
   173→
   174→## Core Principles
   175→
   176→- **Simplicity First**: Make every change as simple as possible. Impact minimal code.
   177→- **No Laziness**: Find root causes. No temporary fixes. Senior developer standards.
   178→- **Minimal Impact**: Changes should only touch what's necessary. Avoid introducing bugs.
   179→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

I need to understand the exact types used in the ADK model.LLM interface to build an OpenAI adapter. Read these files from the Go module cache:

1. ~/go/pkg/mod/google.golang.org/adk@v0.5.0/model/llm.go - The LLM interface, LLMRequest, LLMResponse types

3. ~/go/pkg/mod/google.golang.org/genai@v1.40.0/client.go or similar - The genai.GenerateContentConfig type

- How genai.Content is structured (Role, Parts)

- How genai.Tool / genai.FunctionDeclaration are structured (for tool definitions)
- How genai.GenerateContentConfig works (temperature, etc.)
- The full LLMResponse fields

Also check the fork's converter file to understand the mapping:
Run: gh api repos/jiatianzhao/adk-go-openai/contents/model/openai/converters.go --jq '.content' | base64 -d

This will show how the fork converts between genai types and OpenAI format.

1→// Copyright 2025 Google LLC
     2→//
     3→// Licensed under the Apache License, Version 2.0 (the "License");
     4→// you may not use this file except in compliance with the License.
     5→// You may obtain a copy of the License at
     6→//
     7→//     http://www.apache.org/licenses/LICENSE-2.0
     8→//
     9→// Unless required by applicable law or agreed to in writing, software
    10→// distributed under the License is distributed on an "AS IS" BASIS,
    11→// WITHOUT WARRANTIES OR CONDITIONS OF ANY KIND, either express or implied.
    12→// See the License for the specific language governing permissions and
    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→// Package model defines the interfaces and data structures for interacting with LLMs.
    16→package model
    17→
    18→import (
    19→	"context"
    20→	"iter"
    21→
    22→	"google.golang.org/genai"
    23→)
    24→
    25→// LLM provides the access to the underlying LLM.
    26→type LLM interface {
    27→	Name() string
    28→	GenerateContent(ctx context.Context, req *LLMRequest, stream bool) iter.Seq2[*LLMResponse, error]
    29→}
    30→
    31→// LLMRequest is the raw LLM request.
    32→type LLMRequest struct {
    33→	Model    string
    34→	Contents []*genai.Content
    35→	Config   *genai.GenerateContentConfig
    36→
    37→	Tools map[string]any `json:"-"`
    38→}
    39→
    40→// LLMResponse is the raw LLM response.
    41→// It provides the first candidate response from the model if available.
    42→type LLMResponse struct {
    43→	Content           *genai.Content
    44→	CitationMetadata  *genai.CitationMetadata
    45→	GroundingMetadata *genai.GroundingMetadata
    46→	UsageMetadata     *genai.GenerateContentResponseUsageMetadata
    47→	CustomMetadata    map[string]any
    48→	LogprobsResult    *genai.LogprobsResult
    49→	// Partial indicates whether the content is part of a unfinished content stream.
    50→	// Only used for streaming mode and when the content is plain text.
    51→	// The Runner fully processes only the final non-partial event, partial
    52→	// events are simply forwarded downstream (eg. to UI for display).
    53→	Partial bool
    54→	// Indicates whether the response from the model is complete.
    55→	// Only used for streaming mode.
    56→	TurnComplete bool
    57→	// Flag indicating that LLM was interrupted when generating the content.
    58→	// Usually it is due to user interruption during a bidi streaming.
    59→	Interrupted  bool
    60→	ErrorCode    string
    61→	ErrorMessage string
    62→	FinishReason genai.FinishReason
    63→	AvgLogprobs  float64
    64→}
    65→

    13→// limitations under the License.
    14→
    15→package genai
    16→
    17→import (
    18→	"context"
    19→	"fmt"
    20→	"log"
    21→	"net/http"
    22→	"os"
    23→	"strings"
    24→
    25→	"cloud.google.com/go/auth"
    26→	"cloud.google.com/go/auth/credentials"
    27→	"cloud.google.com/go/auth/httptransport"
    28→)
    29→
    30→// Client is the GenAI client. It provides access to the various GenAI services.
    31→type Client struct {
    32→	clientConfig ClientConfig
    33→	// Models provides access to the Models service.
    34→	Models *Models
    35→	// Live provides access to the Live service.
    36→	Live *Live
    37→	// Caches provides access to the Caches service.
    38→	Caches *Caches
    39→	// Chats provides util functions for creating a new chat session.
    40→	Chats *Chats
    41→	// Files provides access to the Files service.
    42→	Files *Files
    43→	// Operations provides access to long-running operations.
    44→	Operations *Operations
    45→	// FileSearchStores provides access to the File Search Stores service.
    46→	FileSearchStores *FileSearchStores
    47→	// Batches provides access to the Batch service.
    48→	Batches *Batches
    49→	// Tunings provides access to the Tunings service.
    50→	Tunings *Tunings
    51→	// Tokens provides access to the Tokens service.
    52→	AuthTokens *Tokens
    53→}
    54→
    55→// Backend is the GenAI backend to use for the client.
    56→type Backend int
    57→
    58→const (
    59→	// BackendUnspecified causes the backend determined automatically. If the
    60→	// GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_VERTEXAI environment variable is set to "1" or "true", then
    61→	// the backend is BackendVertexAI. Otherwise, if GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_VERTEXAI
    62→	// is unset or set to any other value, then BackendGeminiAPI is used.  Explicitly
    63→	// setting the backend in ClientConfig overrides the environment variable.
    64→	BackendUnspecified Backend = iota
    65→	// BackendGeminiAPI is the Gemini API backend.
    66→	BackendGeminiAPI
    67→	// BackendVertexAI is the Vertex AI backend.
    68→	BackendVertexAI
    69→)
    70→
    71→// The Stringer interface for Backend.
    72→func (t Backend) String() string {
    73→	switch t {
    74→	case BackendGeminiAPI:
    75→		return "BackendGeminiAPI"
    76→	case BackendVertexAI:
    77→		return "BackendVertexAI"
    78→	default:
    79→		return "BackendUnspecified"
    80→	}
    81→}
    82→
    83→// ClientConfig is the configuration for the GenAI client.
    84→type ClientConfig struct {
    85→	// Optional. API Key for GenAI. Required for BackendGeminiAPI.
    86→	// Can also be set via the GOOGLE_API_KEY or GEMINI_API_KEY environment variable.
    87→	// Get a Gemini API key: https://ai.google.dev/gemini-api/docs/api-key
    88→	APIKey string
    89→
    90→	// Optional. Backend for GenAI. See Backend constants. Defaults to BackendGeminiAPI unless explicitly set to BackendVertexAI,
    91→	// or the environment variable GOOGLE_GENAI_USE_VERTEXAI is set to "1" or "true".
    92→	Backend Backend
    93→
    94→	// Optional. GCP Project ID for Vertex AI. Required for BackendVertexAI.
    95→	// Can also be set via the GOOGLE_CLOUD_PROJECT environment variable.
    96→	// Find your Project ID: https://cloud.google.com/resource-manager/docs/creating-managing-projects#identifying_projects
    97→	Project string
    98→
    99→	// Optional. GCP Location/Region for Vertex AI. Required for BackendVertexAI.
   100→	// Can also be set via the GOOGLE_CLOUD_LOCATION or GOOGLE_CLOUD_REGION environment variable.
